blocks.text: HPI: This is a 72 year old female who:
returns for a follow up regarding the right knee. Patient states that her symptoms have unfortunately
returned despite two steroid injections, first in November 2025 and her second in January 2026. She
states that now, she is requiring assistance going from a sitting to standing position secondary to her
pain. She denies any balance issues. She also feels she is losing range of motion over time. She has
read about a jiffy knee procedure and inquires if this is an option today.
Exam findings notable for the following:
Exam: The patient's skin is warm and dry, with no erythema or ecchymosis; motor and sensory function are
intact, and the patient has 0-120 degrees active range of motion about the knee without pain. There is
medial joint-line tenderness. There are no bony deformities. Distal pulses intact.
X-rays: 4 views of the right knee were taken in the office on 10/29/2025. X-rays demonstrate mild
Imaging: MRI right knee done at Pueblo Medical Imaging on 11/4/2025 demonstrates moderate/severe
tricompartmental osteoarthritis worst in the medial compartment with bone-on-bone phenomena. Small joint
effusion. Medial extrusion of the body of the medial meniscus due to osteoarthritis and joint space loss. No
meniscal tear identified. Mucoid degeneration of the ACL. Patchy bone marrow edema within the distal
femur and proximal tibia which is presumably degeneration in nature.
ULTRASOUND: Limited ultrasound right knee done in the office on 11/12/2025. Findings demonstrate

William Ralston McGee, DO (Primary Provider) (Bill Under)
MRI of the right knee was reviewed, demonstrating the following findings: moderate/severe tricompartmental osteoarthritis worst in the
medial compartment with bone-on-bone phenomena. Small joint effusion. Medial extrusion of the body of the medial meniscus due to
osteoarthritis and joint space loss. No meniscal tear identified. Mucoid degeneration of the ACL. Patchy bone marrow edema within the
distal femur and proximal tibia which is presumably degeneration in nature.
Right knee osteoarthritis. X-rays from 10/2025 were reviewed with the patient in detail. I explained that she is a candidate for a
unicompartmental knee arthroplasty given there are only degenerative changes in her medial femorotibial compartment. We discussed
the risks and benefits regarding a mako robot-assisted unicompartmental knee arthroplasty. At this point, the patient has failed
conservative treatment for her knee osteoarthritis and would like to go forward with unicompartmental mako robot-assisted knee
arthroplasty. We will have her evaluated medically and she will undergo risk stratification and lab work prior to surgery. She will also
complete an education course related to total joint surgery prior to the procedure. I will also order a CT scan of the right knee for fitting of
UKA implants. Once these all have been completed we will plan for a right unicompartmental mako robot-assisted knee arthroplasty.
I discussed the risks, alternatives, and benefits to the procedure with the patient. The risks of surgery include, but are not limited to
bleeding, infection, injury to nerves or blood vessels, DVT, PE, and other risks associated with anesthesia including death. The patient
understands these risks and wants to move forward with the surgery. We will schedule this surgery for the patient at their earliest
Orthobiologics refers to products such as tissue, cells, and molecules made by those cells called growth factors that may aid in
Autologous platelet rich plasma (PRP) is the component of blood that has been prepared to maximize the concentration of platelets
and their associated growth factors. The PRP can be introduced into the painful joint or soft tissue to wake up the signaling
between these molecules necessary for healing. Autologous means that your own cells are used, eliminating much of the concern
regarding disease transmission or allergic reaction. PRP can be obtained from a standard blood draw. There is evidence to support
the potential benefits of PRP for some tendon and joint disorders.
Some growth factors are used in orthopedic surgery to aid the healing of bone and soft tissue structures.
Stem cells are cells that have the potential to turn into different types, or lineages, of cells, such as the cells that make up muscle,
tendon, cartilage, and bone. Stem cells produce and release molecules called growth factors that participate in communication
between cells. The richest source of stem cells in the body is the bone marrow. There are basic science studies that support the
ability of stem cells to help with healing and repair. There is ongoing research to establish the efficacy of stem cell delivery at the
PRP therapy starts with drawing your blood and placing the whole blood volume in a centrifuge, which serves to separate the
desired PRP cells into a richly concentrated layer. You will then be positioned to allow access of the joint or area to be injected.
After sterile preparation of the area, the PRP is delivered by injection. Ultrasound is sometimes used to target the exact location for
treatment. PRP can also be administered at the time of surgery. PRP can be planned as a single injection or a series of injections.
Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with degenerative joint disease of the knee can achieve some
measure of pain relief from nonsurgical options like medications, lifestyle modification, exercise and physical therapy, and use of
supportive devices or change in footwear. The importance of maintaining an ideal body weight or losing weight to reduce stress on
the knee joint is a cornestone of nonoperative management.
Expectations: I counseled the patient about the natural history of degenerative joint disease of the knee which typically has
exacerbations and remissions. Many people report that changes in the weather also affect the degree of pain from arthritis. The
knee joint does not heal on its own when there is arthritis. With time, the symptoms of arthritis may get worse. For some,
William Ralston McGee, DO (Primary Provider) (Bill Under)
conservative management may not be not satisfactory. These individuals should consider surgical options.
Contact Office if your knee pain worsens, or if the knee becomes warm or accompanied by a fever.
Contraindications: Verified that patient has no active infection of the knee joint, no systemic bacteremia, no skin infections, no
neuropathic arthritis, no progressive neurological disease, and no other severe medical conditions that outweigh benefits of TKA.
Risks and benefits: I had a lengthy conversation with the patient/family regarding the risks of surgery, which include but are not
limited to infection, persistent pain, blood clots, pulmonary embolus, need for repeat surgery, cardiac events, fat embolus, swelling,
stroke and death; the benefits of surgery; and the alternatives to surgery which include second opinions, delaying surgery, further
conservative care, or alternative medicine. They have had ample opportunity to have all of their questions answered by the medical
team and are encouraged to contact us with any questions, changes or concerns that arise prior to surgery.
William Ralston McGee, DO (Primary Provider) (Bill Under)
